html:
XV

The Aquitania had crept up the river that morning with exasperating caution. It was the day before Christmas. The more impatient compared tedious experiences in customs; hoped goodwill toward men might have percolated through to the baggage inspectors; wondered if they could get Pullman space on the two-forty-five in case they missed the one-twenty.

Her furs muffled high about her throat, Helen Hudson ventured to the frosty rail of B-deck as they passed the Battery, and found the stinging breeze a bit strenuous. She had avoided three consecutive winters, and the fast trip from Nice to New York had not been free of discomfort.

A spluttery scrawl from Joyce accounted for her sudden decision to return. She had read the letter on a stone bench by the sea wall, a stoneвАЩs throw from the Casino pier, one week ago this morningвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It had been that kind of a journey.

вАЬAll packed and waiting for the taxi,вАЭ Joyce had begun. вАЬGoing back to Detroit. Why Detroit, I donвАЩt know, unless because it seems to offer something like anchorage. IвАЩll try to find some employment thereвБ†вАФanything to occupy my mindвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The past month has been a nightmare! Quite unendurable! Last night, Tom struck me a savage blow on the breast with his fistвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ deeply penitent afterwardsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ wailed his remorse like a little childвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But I told him IвАЩd had enoughвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩm through!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He left, early this morning, ashamed and glum. He thinks IвАЩll be here when he comes home tonight, though I made it plain enough I was leavingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I wonder, darling, would it be asking too much of you to come back and be with me for a few weeks. I do need some good counsel, and IвАЩm bitterly lonelyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs a shame to drag you back to Detroit in midwinterвБ†вАФbut could you? IвАЩve nobody but you! I need you desperately!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Cable me at the StatlerвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩll be wild with joy if you say youвАЩll come!вАЭ

It had taken only an hour to decide. Creasing the letter with puzzled fingers, she had risen and walked mechanically along the Promenade des Anglais for fully half a mile; had slowly retraced her steps, past the Negresso and the Ruhl, so deep in her problem she barely noticed the strollers recruited from a dozen nations. By the time she had reached the little park fronting the Jettee, Helen was ready to concede that Joyce had won her case.

She had called the roll of all the alternatives to a return trip. Why not cable Joyce to come over? But noвБ†вАФJoyce had set her heart on finding some employment. She would find none along the Riviera. Joyce wanted to begin life again. She couldnвАЩt do it lounging in Nice. Joyce was bitterly lonely. WellвБ†вАФNice wouldnвАЩt help that very muchвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ After allвБ†вАФshe did owe Joyce some attention. She must go!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was almost as busy an afternoon as the one on which the had impulsively migrated from Bellagio.

Slowly the big ship warped into her slip; sleety, salty hawsers were wound upon huge rumbling spools somewhere deep in her vitals; the covered gangways were hauled up from the dock and opened to the swarm of restless home-comers. Almost everybody had located wide expectant eyes in the steam-breathing crowd that huddled at the openings of the wharf-shed.

Helen felt very much alone. She exchanged tentative goodbyes with the few friends she had made on board; doubtless she would be bumping into them again in the Customs.

Already a nervous little caucus had convened under the big H, halfway down the draughty warehouse where a pile of luggage was rapidly accumulating. Some sat on their trunks with the resigned despair of the shipwrecked on a desert island; someвБ†вАФless experiencedвБ†вАФsquatted before their gaping bags contemplating last minute amendments to their declaration slips.

Young Mrs.¬†Hudson had done a minimum of collecting in Europe, but it was amazing how many things one unwittingly came into possession of in the course of a mere three years of foreign travel. As she neared her letter, she met a pair of officials headed also in that direction and told them that she was hopeful of spending at least part of Christmas Day with relatives in Detroit. She led them to her belongings and indicated which pieces they were to put the stickers on. She could have brought in the Crown jewels, that morning.

To her delight, the three oвАЩclock train was not crowded; but why should it be? It was Christmas Eve! Normal people were at home. The thought depressed her. For a little while, as the train roared through the tunnel, she missed a home as she had never before.

There was a pleasurable excitement, however, in this return to Detroit. She and Joyce had been very close. Whatever of constraint had come upon their comradeship, in that last trying winter when she had attempted so unsuccessfully to keep the girl from utterly ruining herself, JoyceвАЩs urgent need of her drove all that into oblivion.

And there were many other friends in Detroit she would be happy to see againвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The ByrnesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Should she go out to see Mrs.¬†Ashford? Why notвБ†вАФindeed? Mrs.¬†Ashford had been good to herвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It might be thought strange if Doctor HudsonвАЩs widow returned to Detroit and failed to call at BrightwoodвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Perhaps it would serve the purpose if she invited Mrs.¬†Ashford down town for luncheon and a matin√©eвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It wouldnвАЩt be necessary to go out to the hospitalвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Doctor Pyle would be glad to see her, of course; but she would call on the Pyles at their homeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ BesidesвБ†вАФit would be awkward meeting people one scarcely knew any more, and being expected to remember their namesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And as for that glassblower, who meddled in peopleвАЩs affairs and sent spies about to report on their movements, one would be almost sure to encounter him!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Why should he want to blow glass, anyway?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Imagine!вБ†вАФa brain surgeon spending his time in such silly business!

The sky was the colour of wood-ashes. Big snowflakes flattened against the pane, crumbled, edged toward the lower corner of the sash. No matter where they struck and spattered, or how slowly they made their way, sooner or later, quickly or slowly, they eventually arrived in the corner and packed themselves down hard against the othersвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Her thoughts were like that. Let them strike where they wouldвБ†вАФthey all contrived to bring up at one spot. She was impatient about it, tugged herself free of her reveries, returned to the magazine story and reread with utter disinterest the page that had failed to registerвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Would it pay to take the coup√© out of the storage garage? She had not driven on icy streets for so long. Had she lost her nerve? Skidding was dangerousвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She watched a snowflake creep across the paneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Her eyes grew reminiscent, tender. She bit her lip. Her cheeks were flushedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Then, vexed with herself, she shifted her position and took up the magazine with resolution.

Had Joyce been effectually cured of her drinking? Would she drift into her old habits, once she had renewed connections with her Detroit set?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And be brought in, at all hours, maudlin and stupid?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ An unusually big snowflake went scudding across the glass, clawing for a hold, but unable to stand out against the rule for all snowflakes on this paneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ For a brief instant, she was in his arms, and she felt his lips pressed hard against her ownвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She threw down the magazine and rang the bell for a table. A game of solitaire might divert her.

It was pleasant to be back in an American dining-carвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And, all things considered, it would be better to invite Mrs.¬†Ashford to take an afternoon off and come down townвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Our diners were so much nicer than the European ones where the passengers were organized into platoons and given so many minutes to devour each courseвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What a pity she had felt she had to sell her Brightwood stock. Would it make any difference in her relations with Mrs.¬†Ashford?

It was bitterly cold that night as the long train clanked and creaked and screamed along the east bank of the Hudson, and, crossing the bridge at Albany, thrust its black muzzle into the face of a blinding blizzard; but the scurrying landscape, blurry with snow, did not seem so bleakly inhospitable as she had feared. In her berth, she raised on one elbow, drew aside the blind, and watched the sleet-trimmed trees and fences skimming by, half glad she was returning. She snuggled down with her furs against her cheek, and drifted off into an extended debate with somebody, whether she wasnвАЩt under a sort of obligation to call at Brightwood, if only for a moment.

Joyce was at the gates, dancing gleefully, arms outstretched, as she sighted her beloved emerging from the concourse tunnel, pursued by two porters staggering under bags plastered with foreign labels. There were some murmured tendernesses, and a moment later they were careening around the circle, tire-chains rattling merrily. Bubbling with excitement, Joyce tried to tell half a dozen stories at once.

вАЬFirst thing I did, darling, when I arrivedвБ†вАФweek agoвБ†вАФI went out to see Nancy Ashford. IsnвАЩt she the dearest thing? But you hardly knew her. WellвБ†вАФsheвАЩs regular!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I went out to Brightwood and told Nancy all about it; how I couldnвАЩt stick it another minute and had had to leave him; and could I possibly find anything to do, just to keep from going crazyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And, what do you think? They had just lost a file-clerk, and would I like to do that for a week or two, to find out how I would react to the discipline of office hours; and, meanwhile, we could all be thinking and inquiring for something permanentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But, I donвАЩt mind if nothing turns up for a while. ReallyвБ†вАФit isnвАЩt dull work, at all; and I quite like it!вАЭ

вАЬOh, youвАЩve begun it already?вАЭ

вАЬUmm-humm!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That same afternoon!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Just took off my hat and went to it. HonestlyвБ†вАФitвАЩs a lark. Of course, I knew several of the peopleвБ†вАФDoctor Pyle and Doctor Carter, and the redheaded Watson boy, whoвАЩs grown a moustache and glasses since I saw him last; and fully a dozen of the older nursesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ AndвБ†вАФmy dearвБ†вАФyou should see the Merrick person!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ DonвАЩt frown that way!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I know you never liked him.вАЭ

вАЬYou mean I never knew him.вАЭ

вАЬOf course! That was the whole trouble! You never met him but once, on that awful night! Ugh!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ that night!вАЭ

Helen patted her hand.

вАЬForget it! WeвАЩll never mention it again!вАЭ

Joyce brightened and resumed her monologue.

вАЬWell, as I was saying, all you ever knew about him was that he had a sentimental notion he must study surgery and try to take dear dadвАЩs placeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And you thought it a piece of impudence, didnвАЩt you?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Listen! HeвАЩs going to come near doing it! Do you know what Bobby MerrickвАЩs up and done? HeвАЩs invented a thingвБ†вАФвАЭ

The taxi scraped against the kerb, and the doorman was reaching for the baggage.

вАЬLook! IsnвАЩt this foxy?вАЭ prattled Joyce, as they entered the lobby. вАЬAll done over!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ LetвАЩs go straight through to luncheon. IвАЩm starving!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ThereвАЩs a placeвБ†вАФby the windowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Umm! Duck! ThatвАЩs Christmassy!вАЭ

вАЬWhat were you saying about your work at the hospital, when I interrupted you?вАЭ asked Helen, as the waiter moved away, scribbling on his card.

вАЬWhat was I saying?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh, yesвБ†вАФabout Bobby! HeвАЩs made an electrical thing thatвАЩs bringing head surgeons here from all over. I donвАЩt know exactly what it isвБ†вАФsome sort of a charged knifeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ awfully complicatedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ TheyвАЩre doing operations with it at Brightwood that have never been done anywhere beforeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Something that prevents haemorrhages, or something. Nancy Ashford told me about it; but she couldnвАЩt explain it very well. I saw itвБ†вАФa big, tall, wooden case full of the most intricate machinery you ever sawвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬGlass things?вАЭ

вАЬUmm-humm!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How did you know?вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФthey always do have a lot ofвБ†вАФof things made of glass whenever they use electricity, donвАЩt they?вАЭ

вАЬNaturallyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Have some of that wonderful celery, darlingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Only celery in the world, you knowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And handsome? My word! HonestlyвБ†вАФI wouldnвАЩt have known him! And he always was good-looking!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But the change in him is simply marvellous! IвАЩm half afraid of himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ OhвБ†вАФvery professional! Crisp! No nonsenseвБ†вАФnoвБ†вАФsir-ee! And the nurses are all wild and crazy about himвБ†вАФand he doesnвАЩt know theyвАЩre existingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ HeвАЩs doing some of their most important cases out there, nowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Calls me вАШMrs.¬†Masterson!вАЩ IsnвАЩt that idiotic! Nancy says itвАЩs because IвАЩm employed thereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ FunnyвБ†вАФdonвАЩt you think?вАЭ

Helen thought it was funny; thought the whole speech funny.

вАЬReallyвБ†вАФвАЭ Joyce leaned forward and dropped her voice. вАЬIf I had the teeniest right to, IвАЩd lose my heartвБ†вАФutterly! You know I always was a little bit soft about him!вАЭ

вАЬI hope youвАЩll not be indiscreet, Joyce.вАЭ

вАЬOh, IвАЩll take pains not to let him know I think heвАЩs nice,вАЭ she reassured. вАЬButвБ†вАФI donвАЩt want you feeling unkindly toward him. You really canвАЩt avoid meeting him, you know.вАЭ

вАЬWhy not?вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФyouвАЩll be out at Brightwood, more or less, now IвАЩm thereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt see how your work at Brightwood would require my presence.вАЭ

вАЬButвБ†вАФyouвАЩre going out, arenвАЩt you?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Honestly, darling, youвАЩll just have to be pleasant toward himвБ†вАФfor my sake!вАЭ

вАЬFor your sake?вАЭ

Helen experienced a momentary wave of disappointmentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ In her long absence from Joyce, she had idealized her somewhat; butвБ†вАФshe was the same Joyce, whom no blunders could chasten; the same unchanged Joyce who couldnвАЩt forget anything, or learn anything; hopping gaily out of the frying-pan into the fire.

вАЬYes,вАЭ repeated Joyce dramatically, вАЬfor my sake!вАЭ

вАЬThen,вАЭ said Helen slowly, вАЬIвАЩm sorry I came back.вАЭ

They ate their duck; but it wasnвАЩt very good.

That night they saw The Hypotenuse. It was a rollicking comedy employing a small cast and simple materials. A young widow and her contemporary stepdaughter furtively concealed from each other, throughout the first act, that the late Judge HaskinsвАЩ junior partner was of larger interest than was demanded by his professional service as their attorney and business counsellor. Act Two developed some delicious situations, adroitly handled. The audience was delighted.

When the curtain had finally settled, after several trips in response to warm applause which brought them all back together, and then reconsidered them in relays, finishing with mamma and Polly, hand in hand, Joyce turned animatedly to offer comment and surprised an abstracted look on HelenвАЩs face. She, apparently, had not been quite prepared for the sudden onrush of lights.

вАЬYouвАЩre tired, arenвАЩt you?вАЭ inquired Joyce solicitously.

вАЬIt always takes a day or two, I think, to recover from a long journey.вАЭ

вАЬAwfully jolly show, donвАЩt you think? Fancy such a mess! You and IвБ†вАФfor instance! WeвАЩd be honest with each other, at least! I suppose weвАЩd just cut the deck to see which one was to get him.вАЭ

вАЬStuffy in here, isnвАЩt it?вАЭ said Helen. вАЬLetвАЩs go out and prowl in the foyer. Want to?вАЭ

JoyceвАЩs incapacity for understanding the operations of her own mind was spectacularly displayed, early next morning, as she dressed to go to Brightwood, babbling about herself as a вАЬwoiking goil.вАЭ

Breakfast had been served in their rooms. Helen, exquisite in a dainty lounging robe, was lingering over her coffee and the morning papers.

вАЬI think itвАЩs simply marvellous,вАЭ enthused Joyce, into the mirror, вАЬthat IвАЩve been able to adjust so quickly to office routine, donвАЩt you?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ After all these years of indulging myself, sleeping late, pottering, lazing about! IвАЩm happier than I ever thought I could be again. I know now IвАЩll never be contented, any more, without a regular job.вАЭ

вАЬGlad you like it,вАЭ said Helen, deep in the theatre advertisements. вАЬWhat do people say about this new musical comedy, Jasmine?вАЭ

вАЬVery tuneful, I hearвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Like this hat?вАЭ

вАЬCute!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Suppose Mrs.¬†Ashford might like to come down town, tomorrow, and have dinner with us and see Jasmine?вАЭ

вАЬOh, IвАЩm sure she would love it! IвАЩll ask her. And, darlingвБ†вАФshe and Bobby Merrick are such pals. WouldnвАЩt it be nice to ask him, too?вАЭ

вАЬNot a bit nice! I donвАЩt know him! I donвАЩt want to know him!вАЭ HelenвАЩs tone was frankly impatient.

вАЬWell, you could get acquainted with him. I know him! CouldnвАЩt you consider my wishes a little?вАЭ Joyce savagely flicked probable dust from her coat.

вАЬHand me my pocketbookвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ thereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ on the mantel. Thanks!вАЭ

Helen opened it and unfolded a letter.

вАЬOhвБ†вАФitвАЩs the one I wrote you!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, what about it?вАЭ

вАЬRead itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You will see that you asked me to travel five thousand miles to give you some good advice. Now that IвАЩve gone to the bother of humouring you, I hope youвАЩll not resent it if I say that your present state of mind in regard to Doctor Merrick is absurd!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If you want to be silly about him, donвАЩt annoy me with it! I wonвАЩt have him thrust down my throat!вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФwhatвАЩs come over you? I hadnвАЩt heard that residence in France and Italy made people so squeamish!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And it seems to me that a person with your admiration for people who do really valorous thingsвБ†вАФat a lot of cost to themselvesвБ†вАФwould take a little human interest in Bobby Merrick, slaving himself almost to death, when, if he wanted to, he might be lounging on a yacht somewhere in the Mediterranean!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I heard one of the nurses say he had come into a million dollarsвАЩ worth of Axion Motors when he was twenty-five, and is due for another million when he is thirty! I tell you he deserves some credit!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ GoodbyeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ DonвАЩt be peeved!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ See you about five-thirty.вАЭ

Helen rose, after the door had slammed, and stood looking down upon the streetвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Axion Motors!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A million dollarsвАЩ worth of Axion Motors!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Axion!
